title: Continuous and discontinuous transitions in the Ising-Heisenberg model on the extended Lieb lattice in a magnetic field
abstract: The spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg model on the extended Lieb lattice in a magnetic field is exactly mapped onto an effective spin-1/2 Ising model on the square lattice. The ground-state phase diagram comprises the quantum antiferromagnetic (QAF), quantum monomer-dimer (MD), classical ferrimagnetic (FRI), and classical ferromagnetic phase. The MD-FRI ground-state phase boundary extends to finite temperatures as a dome-shaped surface of discontinuous thermal transitions bounded by a line of Ising critical points. The QAF phase is enclosed by a surface of continuous thermal transitions evolving from the QAF-MD and QAF-FRI ground-state phase boundaries. Monte Carlo simulations fully confirm the existence and nature of both continuous and discontinuous thermal phase transitions obtained by exact and approximate analytical calculations.
summary: This work analyzes phase transitions in the Ising-Heisenberg model on the extended Lieb lattice. By mapping it onto an effective Ising model, the authors map out a complex phase diagram showing both continuous critical lines and dome-shaped regions of discontinuous thermal transitions. The results are strongly confirmed by Monte Carlo simulations, providing a detailed understanding of magnetic ordering under external fields.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature (continuous vs. discontinuous) and location of thermal phase transitions within the spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg model.
main_result: The phase diagram reveals distinct regions characterized by four ground states (QAF, MD, FRI, FM), featuring both dome-shaped surfaces of discontinuous transitions and continuous transition lines.
method: The authors employ an exact mapping technique using the Decoration-Iteration Transformation to reduce the model to an effective Ising model, supplemented by Monte Carlo simulations for verification.
model_or_system: The system is a spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg model defined on the extended Lieb lattice subjected to an external magnetic field. The Hamiltonian involves couplings ($J_1, J$) and the field ($h$).
key_observables: Ground-state phase diagram, finite-temperature phase transitions (continuous vs. discontinuous), magnetization ($\langle m_I angle, \langle m_H angle$), and magnetic susceptibility ($\chi$).
important_parameters: Interaction ratios ($J_1/J$), external field strength ($h/J$), and temperature ($k_B T/J$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on an exact mapping of the complex model onto an effective Ising model, which is rigorously tested against Monte Carlo results.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate global phase diagrams in 3D parameter space ($J_1/J - h/J - k_B T/J$), showing distinct surfaces for continuous (blue) and discontinuous (red) transitions. Isothermal magnetization curves confirm plateaus associated with different phases.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the complex Hamiltonian on the Lieb lattice, deriving an effective Ising model via transformation techniques, mapping out zero-temperature ground states, and finally constructing global phase diagrams showing both continuous and discontinuous thermal transition surfaces.
why_interesting: The detailed characterization of both continuous (critical) and first-order (discontinuous) transitions in a frustrated quantum spin system provides rich insights into the interplay between quantum fluctuations and classical ordering mechanisms relevant to many condensed matter systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Ground-state phase diagram of the spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg model on the extended Lieb lattice in the J1/J–h/J plane together with schematic illustrations of the quantum antiferromagnetic (QAF), the quantum monomer-dimer (MD), and the classical ferrimagnetic (FRI) phases. Arrows indicate the spin orientation. FM denotes the fully saturated ferromagnetic phase. A violet oval represents a singlet- dimer state, whereas a double-colored oval indicates a singlet-dimer-like state.

Fig. 2 displays the overall ground-state phase diagram in the J1/J–h/J plane, which comprises four distinct ground states (18)–(21) separated by discontinuous zero-temperature phase tran- sitions determined by the conditions (22) together with schematic illustrations of the QAF, MD, and FRI phases. The most remarkable spin arrangement occurs in the QAF phase (18), where the Heisenberg spin pairs form dimer-singlet-like state (double-colored ovals) further mediat- ing an effective antiferromagnetic interaction between the Ising spins displaying perfect Néel

Figure 3: Density plots of zero-temperature asymptotic values of the effective inter- action Jeff (a) and the effective field heff (b) of the spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg model on the extended Lieb lattice in the J1/J–h/J plane.

Figure 4: Global phase diagram of the spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg extended Lieb lat- tice in the J1/J-h/J-kBT/J parameter space. The blue lines correspond to continuous phase transitions occurring at zero effective field heff = 0. The red surface represents discontinuous thermal phase transitions extending from the ground-state boundary between the MD and FRI phases and terminating at the line of continuous phase transitions. The blue surface corresponds to continuous thermal phase transitions extending from the QAF-MD and QAF-FRI ground-state phase boundaries, whereby the two surfaces continuously merge into one another at zero effective field heff = 0 as indicated by the blue solid line.

Figure 5: Finite-temperature phase diagrams of the spin-1/2 Ising-Heisenberg model on the extended Lieb lattice in the h/J–kBT/J plane for a few selected values of the interaction ratio: (a) J1/J = 0.5, (b) J1/J = 0.9, (c) J1/J = 1.0, (d) J1/J = 1.01, (e) J1/J = 1.25, and (f) J1/J = 1.50. Blue curves denote continuous phase transitions between the QAF phase and either MD or FRI phase,whereas red curves represent discontinuous transitions between the MD and FRI phases terminating at the Ising critical point shown as a blue circle.
